threats.title: Staking Pool Share Theft via Unadjusted depositBalance
threats.hypothesis_description: The staking pool contracts do not decrement user `depositBalance` when funds are transferred to the rollup or challenge manager. If a pool loses its stake, old depositors retain their balances and can steal funds from new depositors.
threats.investigation_rationale: This is a classic flawed accounting vulnerability in staking pools. The pool transfers tokens out but doesn't track the potential loss of those tokens, allowing old shares to claim new deposits.

proof_of_signal.pattern: depositBalance is not adjusted when tokens are transferred out during protocol moves

preconditions: Pool creates an assertion/edge that is subsequently slashed
A new user deposits funds into the pool after the slash

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Accounting mismatch between token balance and internal ledger
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: depositBalance remains unchanged even if the pool's tokens are slashed by the rollup
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to realize losses or adjust balances proportionally

analysis_questions: Can a user withdraw funds deposited by another user if the pool's stake was slashed?
Is there any mechanism that updates depositBalance when the pool loses its stake?
validation_requirements: Simulate a pool losing its stake, followed by a new deposit and a withdrawal by an old depositor
Verify if the new depositor's funds are stolen

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker deposits into a pool, creates a doomed assertion, waits for the stake to be slashed, then waits for a victim to deposit. The attacker immediately withdraws the victim's tokens using their stale depositBalance.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete loss of new user deposits
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Any user depositing into a previously slashed pool

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for pool behavior after stake slashing

technical_investigation_focus.execution_timing: Requires front-running or waiting for victim deposit
technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as a withdrawal immediately following a deposit

threats.title: Premature Force Inclusion due to Missing Time-based Delay Check
threats.hypothesis_description: `forceInclusion` only verifies that the block delay has passed, completely ignoring the time-based delay (`delaySeconds`).
threats.investigation_rationale: The `ISequencerInbox.MaxTimeVariation` struct includes `delaySeconds`, and `getTimeBounds` uses it, but `forceInclusion` only checks `l1BlockAndTime[0] + delayBlocks_ >= block.number`. This allows force inclusion before the intended time has elapsed if block production is faster than expected.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing check for l1BlockAndTime[1] + delaySeconds_ >= block.timestamp

preconditions: delaySeconds is configured and intended to be enforced
Block production rate fluctuates, causing block delay to expire before time delay

initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Only block-based delay is checked, ignoring time-based delay

analysis_questions: Can force inclusion be triggered before the time delay expires?
Is the time delay intentionally omitted from force inclusion?
validation_requirements: Simulate fast block production and attempt force inclusion before time delay expires

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During a period of fast block production, an attacker force-includes a delayed message before the intended time delay expires, potentially disrupting the sequencer's ordering.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Disruption of sequencer ordering, potential MEV extraction

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for time-based delay enforcement in force inclusion

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible on-chain as premature force inclusion

threats.title: First Edge Stake Permanently Locked on Loss
threats.hypothesis_description: The first layer zero edge created for a claim holds its stake in the `EdgeChallengeManager` contract. If this edge loses, it cannot be refunded, and its stake is never forwarded to the `excessStakeReceiver`, permanently locking the funds.
threats.investigation_rationale: Subsequent rival edges send their stake to `excessStakeReceiver`, but the first edge keeps it in `address(this)`. A losing edge cannot call `refundStake`. Thus, the first edge's stake is stuck forever if it loses.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Stake for first edge is kept in contract and never swept if it loses

preconditions: A validator creates the first layer zero edge for a claim
This edge ultimately loses the challenge

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No mechanism to sweep losing first-edge stakes

analysis_questions: What happens to the stake of the first edge if it loses?
Is there any way to recover funds locked in the EdgeChallengeManager?
validation_requirements: Simulate a challenge where the first edge loses
Verify that its stake remains locked in the contract indefinitely

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An honest validator creates the first edge for a legitimate claim, but an attacker successfully challenges it (e.g., via a bug or superior resources). The honest validator's stake is permanently locked instead of being redistributed.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Permanent loss of stake for the first edge creator if they lose

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for losing first-edge stake recovery

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as accumulating balance in EdgeChallengeManager

threats.title: Delay Buffer Replenishment Stagnation via Frequent Batching
threats.hypothesis_description: Buffer replenishment uses integer division `(elapsed * replenishRateInBasis) / BASIS`. If the sequencer posts batches frequently, `elapsed` is small, causing the replenishment to round down to 0, permanently shrinking the buffer.
threats.investigation_rationale: Rounding errors in accumulator logic can lead to permanent state degradation. If the sequencer posts every block or two, `elapsed` is 1 or 2. If `replenishRateInBasis` is e.g. 100 (1%), `1 * 100 / 10000 = 0`.
deployment_assumptions: `replenishRateInBasis` is configured such that frequent batching causes rounding to zero
threats.config_evidence: No deployment evidence provided for specific replenishRateInBasis value

proof_of_signal.pattern: Integer division rounding down to zero for small elapsed values

preconditions: Sequencer posts batches frequently (small elapsed blocks)
replenishRateInBasis is small enough that elapsed * rate < 10000

analysis_questions: Does the buffer fail to replenish under normal sequencer operation?
Can the force inclusion window be permanently reduced due to this rounding?
validation_requirements: Simulate frequent batch posting with typical replenishRateInBasis
Observe buffer replenishment behavior over time

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The sequencer operates normally, posting batches every few blocks. Due to rounding, the buffer never replenishes after a transient depletion, permanently reducing the force inclusion window and making the sequencer more vulnerable to force inclusion attacks.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Increased operational costs for sequencer due to premature force inclusions
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires admin intervention to reset buffer

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for buffer replenishment with small elapsed values

threats.title: Division by Zero in submitBatchSpendingReport on Arbitrum Host Chains
threats.hypothesis_description: If the sequencer inbox is deployed on an Arbitrum host chain and the L2 base fee drops to 0, `submitBatchSpendingReport` will revert due to division by zero, preventing the sequencer from posting batches.
threats.investigation_rationale: Arbitrum chains can experience very low or zero base fees under certain conditions (e.g., custom Orbit chains). The calculation `l1Fees / block.basefee` does not check for a zero denominator.
deployment_assumptions: Deployed on an Arbitrum host chain (`hostChainIsArbitrum == true`)

proof_of_signal.pattern: Division by block.basefee without zero check

analysis_questions: Can block.basefee reach 0 on the target Arbitrum chain?
Does this division by zero completely block batch posting?
validation_requirements: Simulate batch posting on an Arbitrum chain with 0 base fee

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: On a custom Arbitrum Orbit chain configured with a minimum base fee of 0, during periods of low activity, the base fee drops to 0. The sequencer attempts to post a batch but the transaction reverts, halting L2 progression.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete halt of L2 batch posting
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires base fee to increase (which requires transactions, creating a deadlock)

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for batch posting with 0 base fee on Arbitrum

threats.title: Delay Proof Validation Bypass when Buffer is Synced and Full
threats.hypothesis_description: In `delayProofImpl`, if `buffer.isUpdatable()` returns false, the delay proof is completely ignored and not validated against the delayed accumulator.
threats.investigation_rationale: While a synced and full buffer doesn't strictly need updating, accepting an invalid proof without reverting could mask sequencer bugs or allow malicious sequencers to submit garbage proofs that are accepted as valid.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Validation logic wrapped in conditional that can be false

preconditions: Batch poster submits an invalid delay proof

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No unconditional validation of the proof format

analysis_questions: Can a malicious sequencer exploit this to hide invalid state?
Does this bypass violate any off-chain node assumptions?
validation_requirements: Submit an invalid delay proof when buffer is synced and full

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: A compromised sequencer submits garbage delay proofs when the buffer is full. These are accepted on-chain but may cause off-chain nodes that strictly validate all proofs to halt or fork.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Potential consensus split between on-chain and off-chain nodes

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for invalid delay proofs when buffer is not updatable

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Visible as garbage data in delay proof fields

threats.title: Staker Funds Temporarily Locked During BOLD Upgrade
threats.hypothesis_description: `cleanupOldRollup` caps the number of refunded stakers at 50. Stakers beyond this limit are not refunded during the upgrade and must manually withdraw via patched logic.
threats.investigation_rationale: This bounded iteration is intended to prevent out-of-gas errors, but it leaves some users' funds in a degraded state, requiring manual intervention that may not be well-documented or easy to execute.

proof_of_signal.pattern: Bounded iteration leaving remaining items unprocessed

preconditions: More than 50 active stakers at the time of the BOLD upgrade

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No automated mechanism to refund remaining stakers

analysis_questions: Is the patched old rollup user logic guaranteed to allow withdrawal for all remaining stakers?
What happens to stakers involved in active challenges at the time of upgrade?
validation_requirements: Verify that the last 10 stakers can successfully withdraw using the patched logic

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: During the BOLD upgrade, stakers beyond the 50th index find their funds locked in the old rollup contract. They must navigate the patched logic to recover them, potentially facing unexpected reverts if their state is complex (e.g., active challenge).

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Temporary or permanent lock of staker funds

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for upgrades with >50 stakers

threats.title: Privilege Escalation via Direct Call to RollupAdminLogic
threats.hypothesis_description: Admin functions in `RollupAdminLogic` lack explicit access control modifiers, relying entirely on the proxy's routing. If the proxy is misconfigured, anyone can call these functions to take over the rollup.
threats.investigation_rationale: Defense-in-depth is missing. While the `AdminFallbackProxy` is intended to route admin calls only from the admin, any flaw in the proxy or direct calls to the implementation (if it holds state) could be catastrophic.
deployment_assumptions: Proxy routing is misconfigured or implementation contract is initialized directly

proof_of_signal.pattern: Missing onlyOwner modifier on sensitive admin functions

preconditions: Proxy misconfiguration or direct call to implementation

analysis_questions: Is the AdminFallbackProxy completely foolproof against routing manipulation?
Can the implementation contract be initialized directly to hijack it?
validation_requirements: Attempt to call admin functions directly on the implementation contract

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: An attacker finds a way to bypass the proxy's admin check or initializes the implementation contract directly, gaining the ability to pause the rollup, force-confirm assertions, and steal funds.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Complete protocol takeover and fund theft

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for direct calls to implementation contract

threats.title: BOLDUpgradeAction DoS via inboxMaxCount == 0
threats.hypothesis_description: `StateHashPreImageLookup.get()` uses `inboxMaxCount != 0` to check if a hash exists. If the legitimate state has `inboxMaxCount == 0`, the upgrade will revert.
threats.investigation_rationale: Sentinel value collisions are a common source of edge-case bugs. If the genesis state or a very early state is being upgraded, `inboxMaxCount` might legitimately be 0.
preconditions: The latest confirmed assertion being upgraded has inboxMaxCount == 0

initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: No separate boolean flag for existence

analysis_questions: Can a valid assertion have inboxMaxCount == 0?
Will this block the BOLD upgrade?
validation_requirements: Attempt to execute the BOLD upgrade

threats.preliminary_attack_scenario: The protocol attempts to upgrade to BOLD while the rollup is in its genesis state (inboxMaxCount == 0). The upgrade transaction reverts, preventing the deployment of the new challenge manager.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Delay or failure of protocol upgrade
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Requires creating a new assertion to advance inboxMaxCount > 0 before upgrading

threats.test_coverage_gap: Missing tests for upgrades from genesis state
